title: Fracton Topological Holography
authors: Yu-Tao Hu, Jie-Yu Zhang, Peng Ye
abstract: Topological holography (TH), or SymTFT, realizes symmetries and dualities of a quantum system as boundary data of a topological bulk in one higher dimension. We formulate fracton topological holography (FTH), extending this mechanism from liquid topological orders to fracton stabilizer codes. The construction is organized as a general four-stage framework: prepare the bulk model and compute its excitations, determine boundary data and admissible gapped top boundaries, identify the low-energy preserving operator algebra together with its symmetry, relation, and twist data, and then switch among top boundaries to compare the induced boundary descriptions. As a type-I example, we develop FTH for the X-cube model with smooth and rough top boundaries; for a minimal effective Hamiltonian, both yield transverse-field plaquette Ising models, with exchanged subsystem symmetry and twist data, and the boundary switch is implemented by a linear-depth local unitary sequential quantum circuit (SQC). As a type-II example, we formulate FTH for Haah's cubic code in the Laurent-polynomial stabilizer formalism and analyze the natural $(Z)$ and $(X)$ top boundaries, which induce two two-dimensional qubit systems related locally by exchanging generalized plaquette Ising and transverse-field terms and nonlocally by a symmetry--relation duality. These results show that FTH is a genuine extension of TH to both type-I and type-II fracton orders. FTH therefore provides a concrete framework for organizing and understanding duality, with the prospect of offering a systematic route to new dualities.
summary: This paper introduces Fracton Topological Holography (FTH), an extension of Topological Holography, to study quantum systems with fracton excitations. It establishes a rigorous four-stage framework to analyze how boundary conditions induce dualities in both type-I and type-II fracton models. This offers a systematic route to understanding duality in complex topological phases of matter.
main_problem: To extend the concept of Topological Holography (TH) to systems exhibiting fracton topological orders, leading to Fracton Topological Holography (FTH).
main_result: FTH provides a concrete framework for organizing and understanding duality, successfully extending TH to both type-I (X-cube) and type-II (Haah's cubic code) fracton orders.
method: The authors developed a systematic four-stage framework involving preparing the bulk model, determining boundary data, identifying low-energy preserving operator algebras, and switching between top boundaries to induce duality.
model_or_system: The study uses abstract topological models like the X-cube model (type-I) and Haah's cubic code (type-II) as examples of fracton phases, analyzed using stabilizer formalisms.
key_observables: The low-energy effective Hamiltonian ($	ilde{H}$) on the bottom boundary, and the resulting duality mapping between different top boundary conditions.
important_parameters: The type of fracton order (I or II), and the boundary conditions (smooth vs. rough for X-cube; Z vs. X for Haah's code).
main_assumptions: The framework assumes the existence of a systematic four-stage procedure for constructing the holographic sandwich, and that the low-energy dynamics can be captured by boundary operators.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the FTH framework, showing the holographic sandwich structure and specific operator interchanges/dualities for the X-cube and Haah's code.
paper_structure: The paper is structured around the general four-stage FTH framework, applying it first to the X-cube model to demonstrate a plaquette Ising Kramers-Wannier self-duality, and then to Haah's code to test its applicability to type-II fracton orders.
why_interesting: The systematic development of FTH provides a powerful, general mathematical tool to analyze duality in complex, geometrically constrained quantum phases, which is highly relevant for understanding exotic correlated states.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Fig. 2: Microscopic lattice realization of the 1d Transverse Field Ising Model (TFIM) via 2d toric code topological holography (TH) on a cylinder (x-direction periodic, y-direction open and finite). (a) Under the smooth top boundary: the stabilizer generators are complete four-leg Bv terms, four-edge plaquette Ap terms, three-leg top Bv terms except Bv0. The absence of Bv0 allows to add an e defect on the top boundary. Adding an e on the top boundary realizes a twist in the identified 1d TFIM. The effective physical degrees of freedom on the dynamical bottom boundary are identified on the edges: single edge X acts as ˜Xi, and three-leg Bv acts as the Ising term ˜Zi ˜Zi+1. Fig. 3: Systematic procedure for constructing a (fracton) topological holography. The workflow proceeds as following: after preparing the bulk model (Stage 1), one computes the boundary data and chooses a top boundary that satisfies TO condition (Stage 2), constructs transport operators, identifies the effective Pauli operators, symmetries, relations, lifts relations to twist DOFs (Stage 3), then repeats with another legal top boundary to establish a holographic duality and constructs the switching SQC (Stage 4).
